question_title: Will a National Rally–labeled list win the mayoralty of at least one of Marseille, Lyon, or Toulouse in France’s 2026 municipal elections ?
summary: This question essentially reduces to whether RN can win Marseille, since Lyon (RN at 6-8% in polls) and Toulouse (RN at 8%) are near-impossible for RN. In Marseille, RN candidate Franck Allisio is polling neck-and-neck with incumbent Payan at ~30% each in the first round — unprecedented for a major French city. However, multiple structural barriers make an actual RN win unlikely: (1) the PLM sectoral system requires winning a majority of sector-level races, not just an overall vote plurality; (2) the historical "front républicain" tradition of uniting against far-right in second rounds; (3) RN has never won a top-10 French city; (4) the Kalshi prediction market gives Allisio only 11%. A possible four-way second round (quadrangulaire) could split the anti-RN vote, creating a narrow path to victory, but this scenario is far from certain. Overall probability ~13%.

factors.description: RN has NEVER won a top-10 French city — this would be a completely unprecedented breakthrough in French municipal politics
PLM sectoral system in Marseille requires winning majority of sectors, not just citywide plurality — much harder for RN whose support is geographically concentrated
Historical front républicain tradition: French voters consistently unite against far-right in second rounds, and late/strategic voting breaks against RN
Kalshi prediction market gives Allisio only 11% probability of winning Marseille — informed trader assessment
Unprecedented first-round polling closeness: Allisio at 30% neck-and-neck with Payan in Marseille — strongest RN showing ever in a major city
Drug trafficking and security crisis is #1 voter issue in Marseille, directly benefiting RN platform; Marine Le Pen personally campaigning for Allisio
Possible quadrangulaire (4-way second round) with Payan, Allisio, Vassal, and Delogu all qualifying would badly split anti-RN vote; triangulaire polls show 43%-42% Payan-Allisio
Lyon RN candidate at 6-8% and Toulouse RN candidate at 8% — both far below competitive threshold, effectively zero probability for RN in these cities
Vassal declares she will maintain in second round and refuses to step down, potentially splitting center-right vote to RN's benefit

Let me now look at the Polymarket that was mentioned in the earlier search results (for Lyon, Aulas at 94%).Now let me do the probability calculation.Now let me write the meta-reflection and produce my final forecast.**Forecast: 13% probability that RN wins the mayoralty in at least one of Marseille, Lyon, or Toulouse**

**Key findings:**

- **Marseille is the only realistic path** for RN. Their candidate Franck Allisio polls at ~30%, neck-and-neck with incumbent Payan — unprecedented for RN in a major French city. However, structural barriers (the PLM sectoral system, the front républicain tradition, and the Kalshi market pricing at 11%) make an actual win unlikely.

- **Lyon**: RN candidate Dupalais polls at just 6-8%; Jean-Michel Aulas (LR-RE) dominates at 47%. RN is a non-factor.

- **Toulouse**: RN candidate Leonardelli polls at 8%, below the 10% second-round qualification threshold. The race is between Moudenc (center-right) and Briançon (left coalition).

- **The key wildcard in Marseille** is a potential four-way second round (quadrangulaire) that could split the anti-RN vote. Second-round polls show an extremely tight Payan 43% vs Allisio 42% in one triangulaire scenario — but the sectoral system and late strategic voting make a citywide vote share insufficient to win the mayoralty.

resolution_reason: The 2026 French municipal elections concluded on March 22, 2026. In all three specified cities: (1) Marseille - Benoît Payan (Union de gauche) won with ~54% over RN's Franck Allisio (~40%); (2) Lyon - Grégory Doucet (Union de gauche/Greens) won with ~51% over Jean-Michel Aulas (centrist) - RN was not in the runoff; (3) Toulouse - Jean-Luc Moudenc (divers droite/non-RN right) won with ~54% over François Piquemal (LFI) - RN candidate received only ~5% in the first round. No RN-labeled list won the mayoral election in any of these three cities. While RN and allies won in other cities like Nice (Éric Ciotti), that city is not part of the resolution criteria.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves as “Yes” if the official municipal results published show that the winning mayoral list in at least one of Marseille, Lyon, or Toulouse is labeled/nuanced as National Rally (Rassemblement National, “RN”) on the French Ministry of the Interior elections site or [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions).
fine_print: This resolves based on the final outcome of the municipal election (including second round if held). If the Ministry site uses a “nuance” code, treat RN-coded lists as qualifying; do not include broader far-right labels unless explicitly RN-coded. If all three cities’ winning lists are not RN-labeled, resolve “No”.
background_info:
This tracks whether RN converts national-level support into executive control in a major city, which is strategically important for legitimacy, patronage, and media salience.
